---
title: "V2 Migration Guide"
description: "Migrate legacy template definitions to the current Go SDK workflow."
---

# V2 Migration Guide

There are three practical ways to migrate an existing legacy template definition to the current Go SDK workflow:

- use the migration command
- parse your existing Dockerfile with `FromDockerfile(...)`
- keep building a Docker image yourself and reference it with `FromImage(...)`

The default E2B template user is `user`, with `/home/user` as the working directory. This differs from plain Docker defaults, and the migration paths below account for that behavior.

## Migration command

The recommended path is the E2B CLI migration flow.

1. Install the latest E2B CLI.
2. Go to the folder that contains your legacy `e2b.toml` and `e2b.Dockerfile`.
3. Run `e2b template migrate`.
4. Follow the prompts.

Current generated Go output is typically:

- `template.go`
- `build_dev.go`
- `build_prod.go`

## Use `FromDockerfile(...)`

If you want to keep the Dockerfile as the source of truth, parse it into the Go template builder.

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

var dockerfileContent = `
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENTRYPOINT ["npm", "start"]
`

var template = e2b.Template(nil).FromDockerfile(dockerfileContent)
```

You can also parse a Dockerfile from disk:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

var template = e2b.Template(&e2b.TemplateOptions{
	FileContextPath: ".",
}).FromDockerfile("Dockerfile")
```

Current compatible instructions in the Go parser:

- `FROM`
- `RUN`
- `COPY`
- `ADD`
- `WORKDIR`
- `USER`
- `ENV`
- `ARG`
- `CMD`
- `ENTRYPOINT`

Multi-stage Dockerfiles are not supported.

After the template definition is in place, build it the same way as shown in [Quickstart](/docs/template/quickstart).

## Use `FromImage(...)`

If you already build and publish your own Docker image, use that image directly as the template base.

Build the image for `linux/amd64`, push it to your registry, then reference the tag from Go:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

var template = e2b.Template(nil).FromImage("image-tag")
```

If the image is in a private registry, provide credentials as shown in [Private Registries](/docs/template/private-registries).

For more detail on Dockerfile parsing and image-based templates, see [Base Image](/docs/template/base-image).
